497	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
498	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
499	% NOTE: Declarations in this subsection are currently \texttt{private} in the Lean
500	% source; \lean{} pins may not resolve until they are moved to a dedicated module.
501	
502	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
503	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
504	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
505	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
506	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
507	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
508	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
509	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
510	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
511	
512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
513	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
514	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
516	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
517	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
518	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
519	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
520	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
521	  \(F\):
522	  \[
523	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
524	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
525	  \]
526	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
527	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
528	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
529	  the Cauchy product.
530	\end{lemma}
531	
532	\begin{lemma}\leanok
533	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
534	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
535	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
536	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
537	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
538	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
539	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
540	  \[
541	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
542	  \]
543	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
544	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
545	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
546	  \[
547	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
548	  \]
549	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
550	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
551	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
552	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
553	\end{lemma}
554	
555	\begin{proof}
556	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
557	  \leanok
558	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
559	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
560	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
561	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
562	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
563	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
564	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
565	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
566	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
567	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
568	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
569	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
570	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
571	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
572	\end{proof}
573	
574	\begin{definition}\leanok
575	[Rational Hilbert function]
576	  \label{def:ratHilb}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
578	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
579	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
580	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
581	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
582	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
583	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
584	  \[
585	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
586	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
587	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
588	  \]
589	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
590	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
591	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
592	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
593	\end{definition}
594	
595	\begin{lemma}\leanok
596	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
597	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
598	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
599	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
600	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
601	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
602	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
603	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
604	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
605	\end{lemma}
606	
607	\begin{lemma}\leanok
608	[Raising the pole order]
609	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
610	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
611	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
612	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
613	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
614	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
615	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
616	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
617	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
618	\end{lemma}
619	
620	\begin{lemma}\leanok
621	[Closure under difference]
622	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
623	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
624	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
625	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
626	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
627	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
628	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
629	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
630	\end{lemma}
631	
632	\begin{lemma}\leanok
633	[Closure under right shift]
634	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
636	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
637	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
638	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
639	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
640	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
641	  Lean.
642	\end{lemma}
643	
644	\begin{lemma}\leanok
645	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
646	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
647	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
648	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
649	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
650	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
651	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
652	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
653	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
654	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
655	\end{lemma}
656	
657	\begin{proof}
658	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
659	  \leanok
660	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
661	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
662	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
663	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
664	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
665	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
666	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
667	\end{proof}
668	
669	\begin{lemma}\leanok
670	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
671	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
673	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
674	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
675	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
676	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
677	  \[
678	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
679	  \]
680	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
681	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
682	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
683	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
684	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
685	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
686	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
687	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
688	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
689	\end{lemma}
690	
691	\begin{proof}
692	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
693	  \leanok
694	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
695	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
696	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
697	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
698	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
699	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
700	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
701	\end{proof}
702	
703	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
704	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
705	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
706	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
707	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
708	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
709	  % Polynomials" (read from
710	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
711	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
712	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
713	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
714	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
715	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
716	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
717	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
718	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
719	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
720	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
721	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
722	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
723	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
724	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
725	  \[
726	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
727	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
728	  \]
729	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
730	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
731	\end{theorem}
732	
733	\begin{proof}
734	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
735	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
736	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
737	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
738	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
739	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
740	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
741	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
742	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
743	  function
744	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
745	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
746	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
747	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
748	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
749	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
750	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
751	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
752	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
753	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
754	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
755	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
756	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
757	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
758	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
759	\end{proof}
760	
761	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
762	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
763	
764	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
765	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
766	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
767	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
768	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
769	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
770	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
771	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
772	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
773	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
774	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
775	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
776	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
777	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
778	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
779	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
780	
781	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
782	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
783	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
784	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
785	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
786	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
787	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
788	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
789	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
790	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
791	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
792	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
793	
794	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
795	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
796	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
797	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
798	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
799	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
800	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
801	\(x \in R_1\).
802	
803	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}
804	
805	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
806	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
807	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
808	  \mathlibok
809	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
810	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
811	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
812	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
813	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
814	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
815	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
816	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure; the ambient internal
817	  direct-sum decomposition \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\) is supplied by the split
818	  \(G_1\) (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
819	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}).
820	\end{lemma}
821	
822	\begin{lemma}[A span of homogeneous elements is homogeneous]
823	  \label{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}
824	  \lean{Ideal.homogeneous_span}
825	  \mathlibok
826	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
827	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Ideal}).}
828	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(s \subseteq R\) a set each of
829	  whose elements is homogeneous. Then the ideal \(\mathrm{span}\,s\) is a
830	  homogeneous ideal. In particular, for \(x \in R_1\) the principal ideal \((x)\)
831	  is homogeneous, hence \(xM\) and the auxiliary submodule \(N' + x \cdot N\) are
832	  homogeneous; this homogeneity feeds the kernel/cokernel closure
833	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
834	\end{lemma}
835	
836	\begin{lemma}[Rank--nullity]
837	  \label{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}
838	  \lean{LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker}
839	  \mathlibok
840	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
841	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.FiniteDimensional.Lemmas}).}
842	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
843	  \(\varphi : V \to W\) a \(\kappa\)-linear map. Then
844	  \[
845	    \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
846	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
847	  \]
848	  This is the rank--nullity identity underlying the degreewise difference identity
849	  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}).
850	\end{lemma}
851	
852	\begin{lemma}[Finite generation transfers along a surjection]
853	  \label{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
854	  \lean{Submodule.FG.restrictScalars_of_surjective}
855	  \mathlibok
856	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic}).}
857	  Let \(\varphi : R \to A\) be a surjective ring homomorphism and \(M\) an
858	  \(A\)-module that is finitely generated when viewed as an \(R\)-module via
859	  \(\varphi\). Then \(M\) is finitely generated as an \(A\)-module. Applied to the
860	  surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots,
861	  t_{r-2}]\) killing the endomorphism \(x = t_{r-1}\) that annihilates the two
862	  subquotients, this transfers their finite generation down one generator
863	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
864	\end{lemma}
865	
866	\begin{lemma}[Multiplication by a homogeneous element shifts degree]
867	  \label{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
868	  \lean{SetLike.IsHomogeneousElem.graded_smul}
869	  \mathlibok
870	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GradedMulAction}).}
871	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a graded family acting on a graded family \(\mathcal{M}\)
872	  via a graded scalar multiplication. If \(a \in R\) is homogeneous of degree
873	  \(i\) -- in particular \(x \in R_1\) -- then for \(m \in M_n\) one has
874	  \(a \cdot m \in M_{i+n}\). This is the degree-shifting property that makes \(xM\)
875	  a homogeneous submodule (with degree-\((n+1)\) part \(x \cdot M_n\)) and that
876	  underlies the ambient identity \(x \cdot N \cap M_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap M_n)\)
877	  used in the subquotient closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) and
878	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}).
879	\end{lemma}
880	
881	\begin{lemma}[Commutative subalgebra generated by commuting elements]
882	  \label{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
883	  \lean{Algebra.isMulCommutative_adjoin}
884	  \mathlibok
885	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
886	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Algebra.Subalgebra.Lattice}).}
887	  Let \(R\) be a commutative semiring and \(A\) an \(R\)-algebra, possibly
888	  noncommutative. If \(s \subseteq A\) is a set of pairwise-commuting elements
889	  (\(a b = b a\) for all \(a, b \in s\)), then the \(R\)-subalgebra
890	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_R(s)\) generated by \(s\) has commutative multiplication; the
891	  commutative-ring structure on it follows. Applied to the \(r\) pairwise-commuting degree-raising endomorphisms
892	  \(x_0, \dots, x_{r-1} \in \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\), it yields a
893	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots,
894	  x_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); the polynomial action of
895	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) on \(M\) factors through this commutative \(A\)
896	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
897	\end{lemma}

899	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness transfers along a surjective linear map]
900	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}
901	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_surjective}

905	  surjective \(A\)-linear map. If \(M\) is a finite \(A\)-module, then so is \(N\).
906	  This is the finiteness-transfer step at the heart of the ambient finiteness
907	  transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) and of the

910	  \cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}).

914	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_injective_mathlib}

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[Left Cramer inverse]
83	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
84	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
88	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
89	\end{lemma}
90	
91	\begin{lemma}
92	[Right Cramer inverse]
93	  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
94	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
98	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	[Universal property of an away-localisation]
103	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
104	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
108	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
109	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
110	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
114	
115	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
116	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
117	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
118	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
119	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).
120	
121	\begin{definition}
122	
123	\leanok
124	[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
125	  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
127	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
128	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
129	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
130	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
132	  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
133	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
134	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
135	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
136	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
137	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
138	  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
139	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
140	  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates
141	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\), \(q \notin I\)). It is the universal point of
142	  the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}): a ring map
143	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
144	  with identity \(I\)-minor.
145	\end{definition}
146	
147	\begin{definition}
148	
149	\leanok
150	[The minor determinant \(P^I_J\)]
151	  \label{def:gr_minor_det}
152	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.minorDet}
153	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
154	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
155	  % affine patches" (read from
156	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
158	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
159	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
160	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$."
161	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
162	  For \(J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(J) = d\), let \(X^I_J\) denote the
163	  \(J\)-th minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix})
164	  -- the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose columns are those indexed by \(J\). Set
165	  \[
166	    P^I_J \;:=\; \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
167	  \]
168	  Then \(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) (the localisation of the chart ring
169	  away from \(P^I_J\)) is the ring of the principal open subscheme
170	  \(U^I_J = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J] \subseteq U^I\) on which \(P^I_J\) is
171	  invertible. One has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\), so \(R^I_I =
172	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) and \(U^I_I = U^I\).
173	\end{definition}
174	
175	\begin{definition}
176	
177	\leanok
178	[The localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\) over \(R^I_J\)]
179	  \label{def:gr_universal_minor}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinor}
181	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_minor_det}
182	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
183	  % affine patches" (read from
184	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
185	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
186	  % "Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
187	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible."
188	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
189	  Write \(X^I_J \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) for the \(J\)-minor of
190	  the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) with its entries
191	  pushed forward along the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\) into the
192	  localised chart ring \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\)
193	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}). By construction \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of
194	  \(P^I_J\) in \(R^I_J\).
195	\end{definition}
196	
197	\begin{lemma}
198	
199	\leanok
200	[The localised minor determinant is a unit]
201	  \label{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_det_universalMinor}
203	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
205	  % affine patches" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
209	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
210	  %  on $U^I_J$."
211	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
212	  The determinant of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
213	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}) is a unit of \(R^I_J\):
214	  \[
215	    \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \in (R^I_J)^{\times}.
216	  \]
217	\end{lemma}
218	\begin{proof}
219	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
220	  \leanok

222	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) under the structure map
223	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\). Since \(R^I_J\) is the localisation away from
224	  \(P^I_J\), that image is a unit by
225	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}.
226	\end{proof}
227	
228	\begin{definition}
229	
230	\leanok
231	[The Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\)]
232	  \label{def:gr_universalMinorInv}
233	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv}
234	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor}
235	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
236	  % affine patches" (read from
237	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).

242	  Let \((X^I_J)^{-1} \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) be the

244	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}). Its entries lie in \(R^I_J\) by Cramer's rule.
245	\end{definition}
246	
247	\begin{lemma}
248	
249	\leanok
250	[The Cramer inverse is a two-sided inverse]
251	  \label{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv_mul_cancel}

255	  the Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) is a
256	  genuine two-sided inverse:
257	  \[
258	    (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}
259	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
260	    X^I_J\, (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}.
261	  \]
262	\end{lemma}

267	  \cref{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul} (left identity) and
268	  \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv} (right identity), both of which apply once
269	  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is known to be a unit.
270	\end{proof}
271	
272	\begin{definition}
273	
274	\leanok
275	[The image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)]
276	  \label{def:gr_image_matrix}
277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix}
278	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_universal_matrix}
279	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together

290	  \]

302	\leanok

308	  % affine patches" (read from

326	\begin{lemma}
327	
328	\leanok
329	[The pre-hom sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit]
330	  \label{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}
331	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
332	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
333	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
334	  % affine patches" (read from
335	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
336	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
337	  % "In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
338	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
339	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
340	  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
341	  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
342	  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
343	  \[
344	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
345	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
346	  \]
347	  which is a unit.
348	\end{lemma}
349	\begin{proof}
350	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
351	  \leanok
352	  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
353	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
354	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
355	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
356	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
357	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
358	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
359	\end{proof}
360	
361	\begin{definition}
362	
363	\leanok
364	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
365	  \label{def:gr_transition}
366	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
367	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_affine_chart}
368	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
369	  % affine patches" (read from
370	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
371	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
372	  % "For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
373	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
374	  %  defined as follows. ...
375	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
376	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
377	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
378	  Because the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to
379	  R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit of \(R^I_J\)
380	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}), the universal property of the
381	  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
382	  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
383	  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
384	  \[
385	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
386	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
387	  \]
388	  Dually \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
389	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), which we also denote
390	  \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the universal matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\).
391	\end{definition}
392	
393	\begin{lemma}
394	
395	\leanok
396	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
397	  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
398	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
399	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
400	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
401	  % affine patches" (read from
402	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
403	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
404	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
405	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
406	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
407	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
408	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
409	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
410	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
411	\end{lemma}
412	\begin{proof}
413	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
414	  \leanok
415	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
416	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
417	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
418	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
419	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
420	\end{proof}
421	
422	\subsection*{Project-local matrix and minor bookkeeping}
423	
424	The cocycle computation rests on a handful of elementary matrix-algebra
425	identities over the (localised) chart rings. They are project-internal helpers
426	with no external source; each is verified directly in Lean.
427	
428	\begin{lemma}
429	
430	\leanok
431	[Column submatrix of a product]
432	  \label{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}
433	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.mul_submatrix_col}
434	  Let \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
435	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(R)\) be matrices over a commutative ring,
436	  and \(g : \{1,\dots,d\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) a column-index map. Then taking the
437	  column submatrix of the product equals multiplying by the column submatrix of
438	  the right factor:
439	  \[
440	    (A B)_{\bullet\, g} \;=\; A \,\bigl(B_{\bullet\, g}\bigr).
441	  \]
442	\end{lemma}
443	\begin{proof}
444	
445	\leanok
446	  Entrywise from the definition of matrix multiplication (reindexing the rows by
447	  the identity leaves the contraction index untouched). Proved directly in Lean.
448	\end{proof}
449	
450	\begin{lemma}
451	
452	\leanok
453	[A ring map commutes with the Cramer inverse]
454	  \label{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_nonsing_inv}
456	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
457	  Let \(f : R \to S\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
458	  \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{n\times n}(R)\) a square matrix with \(\det A\) a
459	  unit. Writing \(f_\ast\) for the entrywise application of \(f\), the
460	  nonsingular inverse is natural:
461	  \[
462	    \bigl(f_\ast A\bigr)^{-1} \;=\; f_\ast\bigl(A^{-1}\bigr).
463	  \]
464	\end{lemma}
465	\begin{proof}
466	
467	\leanok
468	  Since \(f_\ast A \cdot f_\ast(A^{-1}) = f_\ast(A A^{-1}) = f_\ast(1_{n\times n})
469	  = 1_{n\times n}\) (using \(A A^{-1} = 1\), \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}),
470	  the matrix \(f_\ast(A^{-1})\) is a right inverse of \(f_\ast A\), hence equals
471	  \((f_\ast A)^{-1}\). Proved directly in Lean.
472	\end{proof}
473	
474	\begin{lemma}
475	
476	\leanok
477	[Functoriality of entrywise application]
478	  \label{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_map_eq_of_comp}
480	  Let \(f : R \to A\), \(g : A \to D\) and \(h : R \to D\) be ring
481	  homomorphisms with \(g \circ f = h\). Then for any matrix \(M\) over \(R\),
482	  applying \(f\) then \(g\) entrywise equals applying \(h\) entrywise:
483	  \(g_\ast(f_\ast M) = h_\ast M\).
484	\end{lemma}
485	\begin{proof}
486	
487	\leanok
488	  Immediate from functoriality of \(\operatorname{Mat}(-)\) (composition of
489	  entrywise maps). Proved directly in Lean.
490	\end{proof}
491	
492	\begin{lemma}
493	
494	\leanok
495	[Inverse cancellation in a composite]
496	  \label{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
497	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
498	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
499	  Let \(A, B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
500	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(R)\) over a commutative ring, with
501	  \(\det A\) a unit. Then
502	  \[
503	    \bigl(B^{-1} A\bigr)\,\bigl(A^{-1} M\bigr) \;=\; B^{-1} M.
504	  \]
505	\end{lemma}
506	\begin{proof}
507	
508	\leanok
509	  By associativity and \(A A^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\)
510	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}). Proved directly in Lean.
511	\end{proof}
512	
513	\begin{lemma}
514	
515	\leanok
516	[The \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity]
517	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
518	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
519	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
520	  The \(I\)-minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\)
521	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), with its \(I\)-columns read through the order
522	  isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\), is the \(d\times d\)
523	  identity: \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\). (This is the normalisation \(P^I_I = 1\)
524	  defining the chart.)
525	\end{lemma}
526	\begin{proof}
527	
528	\leanok
529	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix} the \(q\)-column with \(q \in I\) is the
530	  standard basis vector \(e_p\) where \(p\) is the preimage of \(q\) under the
531	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\); so the \(I\)-minor
532	  is the identity. Proved directly in Lean.
533	\end{proof}
534	
535	\begin{lemma}
536	
537	\leanok
538	[The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix is the identity]
539	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
540	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
541	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
542	  The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
543	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the identity:
544	  \[
545	    M_J \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J \;=\; 1_{d\times d}.
546	  \]
547	\end{lemma}
548	\begin{proof}
549	
550	\leanok
551	  The \(J\)-column submatrix of \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is
552	  \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) times the \(J\)-column submatrix of \(X^I\), i.e.
553	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J\), by \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}; this is
554	  \(1_{d\times d}\) by the left-inverse identity
555	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}). Proved directly in Lean.
556	\end{proof}
557	
558	\begin{lemma}
559	
560	\leanok
561	[The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix is the Cramer inverse]
562	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
563	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
564	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
565	  The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
566	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the Cramer inverse:
567	  \[
568	    M_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}.
569	  \]
570	\end{lemma}
571	\begin{proof}
572	
573	\leanok
574	  By \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col} the \(I\)-column submatrix of \(M\) is
575	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\), and \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\)
576	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
577	\end{proof}
578	
579	\begin{lemma}
580	
581	\leanok
582	[\(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) realises the matrix formula]
583	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
584	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
585	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
586	  Applying the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
587	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) entrywise to the universal matrix \(X^J\) yields
588	  the image matrix:
589	  \[
590	    (\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast\, X^J \;=\; M \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
591	  \]
592	\end{lemma}
593	\begin{proof}
594	
595	\leanok
596	  On a free entry \(x^J_{p,q}\) (\(q \notin J\)) this holds by the definition of
597	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) as evaluation at the entries of \(M\)
598	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}). On a column \(q \in J\) the entry of \(X^J\) is
599	  an identity-block entry, which \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries to the
600	  corresponding entry of \(M_J = 1_{d\times d}\)
601	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
602	\end{proof}
603	
604	\begin{lemma}
605	
606	\leanok
607	[Image of a cross minor under \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)]
608	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
609	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap_minorDet}
610	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
611	  For a third size-\(d\) subset \(K\), the pre-hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) sends the
612	  minor determinant \(P^J_K\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) to the determinant of the
613	  \(K\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\):
614	  \[
615	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_K\bigr) \;=\; \det\bigl(M_K\bigr).
616	  \]
617	\end{lemma}
618	\begin{proof}
619	
620	\leanok
621	  The hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) commutes with determinants and with passing to
622	  a column submatrix, so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) =
623	  \det\bigl((\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast X^J\bigr)_K\); apply
624	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}. Proved directly in Lean.
625	\end{proof}
626	
627	\begin{lemma}
628	
629	\leanok
630	[Naturality of the image matrix]
631	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}
632	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_map_eq}
633	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_universal_minor}
634	  Let \(X\) be a size-\(d\) subset and \(\iota : R^I_X \to D\) a ring
635	  homomorphism lying over the structure map \(R^I \to D\), i.e.\
636	  \(\iota \circ (R^I \to R^I_X) = (R^I \to D)\). Writing \(Y\) for the universal
637	  matrix \(X^I\) base-changed to \(D\) and \(Y_X\) for its \(X\)-minor, one has
638	  \[
639	    \iota_\ast\bigl((X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\bigr) \;=\; (Y_X)^{-1}\, Y .
640	  \]
641	\end{lemma}
642	\begin{proof}
643	
644	\leanok
645	  Distribute \(\iota_\ast\) across the product \(M = (X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\); the
646	  factor \((X^I_X)^{-1}\) passes through \(\iota_\ast\) as a Cramer inverse
647	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}, using \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), and both
648	  factors collapse to base changes over \(D\) by functoriality
649	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}) since \(\iota\) lies over \(R^I \to D\).
650	  Proved directly in Lean.
651	\end{proof}
652	
653	\subsection*{Triple-overlap rings and the localised transition maps}
654	
655	The cocycle condition \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) cannot
656	be stated as a naive composition of the landed transition maps of
657	\cref{def:gr_transition}: the codomain of \(\theta_{J,K}\) (the ring
658	\(R^J[1/P^J_K]\)) differs from the domain of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (the ring
659	\(R^J[1/P^J_I]\)). The identity therefore lives over the \emph{triple-overlap}
660	rings obtained by inverting both relevant minors in each chart ring:
661	\[
662	  S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)], \quad
663	  S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)], \quad
664	  S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)].
665	\]
666	
667	\begin{definition}
668	
669	\leanok
670	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (left)]
671	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_left}
672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft}
673	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
674	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
675	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} : R[1/x] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
676	  \(y\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
677	  the structure map \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(x\) — well-defined because \(x\)
678	  is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\) (a factor of the unit \(xy\),
679	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
680	\end{definition}
681	
682	\begin{definition}
683	
684	\leanok
685	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (right)]
686	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_right}
687	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight}
688	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
689	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
690	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} : R[1/y] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
691	  \(x\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
692	  \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(y\) — well-defined since \(y\) is a unit of
693	  \(R[1/(xy)]\) (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
694	\end{definition}
695	
696	\begin{lemma}
697	
698	\leanok
699	[Left inclusion lies over \(R\)]
700	  \label{lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
701	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
702	  \uses{def:gr_away_incl_left}
703	  The inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left})
704	  intertwines the two structure maps:
705	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} \circ (R \to R[1/x]) = (R \to R[1/(xy)])\).
706	\end{lemma}
707	\begin{proof}
708	
709	\leanok
710	  By the defining property of the away-localisation lift
711	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}). Proved directly in Lean.
712	\end{proof}
713	
714	\begin{lemma}
715	
716	\leanok
717	[Right inclusion lies over \(R\)]
718	  \label{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}
719	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}
720	  \uses{def:gr_away_incl_right}
721	  The inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right})
722	  intertwines the two structure maps:
723	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} \circ (R \to R[1/y]) = (R \to R[1/(xy)])\).
724	\end{lemma}
725	\begin{proof}
726	
727	\leanok
728	  By the defining property of the away-localisation lift
729	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}). Proved directly in Lean.
730	\end{proof}
731	
732	\begin{lemma}
733	
734	\leanok
735	[Left factor is a unit in the double localisation]
736	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}
737	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}
738	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
739	  For \(x, y \in R\), the image of \(x\) in \(R[1/(xy)]\) is a unit.
740	\end{lemma}
741	\begin{proof}
742	
743	\leanok
744	  \(xy\) is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\)
745	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}); a factor of a unit is a unit.
746	  Proved directly in Lean.
747	\end{proof}
748	
749	\begin{lemma}
750	
751	\leanok
752	[Right factor is a unit in the double localisation]
753	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}
754	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}
755	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
756	  For \(x, y \in R\), the image of \(y\) in \(R[1/(xy)]\) is a unit.
757	\end{lemma}
758	\begin{proof}
759	
760	\leanok
761	  \(xy\) is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\)
762	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}); a factor of a unit is a unit.
763	  Proved directly in Lean.
764	\end{proof}
765	
766	\begin{lemma}
767	
768	\leanok
769	[The cross minor becomes a unit after inclusion]
770	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
771	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
772	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, def:gr_image_matrix}
773	  Let \(A, B, C\) be size-\(d\) subsets and \(\iota : R^A_B \to D\) a ring
774	  homomorphism over the structure map \(R^A \to D\) under which the minor
775	  determinant \(P^A_C\) becomes a unit of \(D\). Then
776	  \(\iota\bigl(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C)\bigr)\) is a unit of \(D\). Indeed
777	  \[
778	    \tilde\theta_{A,B}\bigl(P^B_C\bigr)
779	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1} X^A_C\bigr)
780	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1}\bigr)\cdot P^A_C ,
781	  \]
782	  a product of two units once \(P^A_C\) is inverted in \(D\).
783	\end{lemma}
784	\begin{proof}
785	
786	\leanok
787	  By \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet},

926	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
927	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
928	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
929	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
930	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
931	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
932	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).

940	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
941	  \[

948	  \[
949	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
950	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
951	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,

964	\section{Scheme-level glue-data layer}
965	\label{sec:gr_glue_layer}
966	
967	The transition data of \cref{sec:gr_transition} lives at the level of rings. To
968	feed it to a scheme-gluing machine we must re-express each piece as a morphism in
969	the category of schemes: the charts \(U^I\), the principal-open overlaps
970	\(U^I_J\), their inclusions, and the transitions \(\theta_{I,J}\) all become
971	schemes and scheme morphisms by applying the contravariant functor \(\Spec\).
972	The lemmas of this section record those translations together with the two
973	genuinely geometric facts needed to assemble a glue datum: that each inclusion
974	\(U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\) is an open immersion, and that the fibre product of
975	two principal opens of \(\Spec A\) is again a principal open. They are
976	project-internal bridge results with no external source beyond the gluing
977	construction of \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}.
978
